% --- Frontmatter ---
\title[A Salem--Spencer constraint benchmark]{Salem--Spencer sets as a
  constraint-solving benchmark: decomposition, certification, and split-policy
  effects at $\rthree{212}$}

\author{Mehmet Ergezer}
\thanks{ORCID: \href{https://orcid.org/0000-0001-6627-3667}{0000-0001-6627-3667}.}
\address{Wentworth Institute of Technology, Boston, MA, USA}
\email{ergezerm@wit.edu}
% \author{Co-author Name}     % add as needed
% \address{Institution}
% \email{email}

\subjclass[2020]{
  Primary 11B25;
  Secondary 11Y16, 68W30, 68V20, 90C10
}

\keywords{
  constraint programming,
  cube decomposition,
  implied constraints,
  certified solving,
  benchmark instances,
  arithmetic progressions
}

\date{July 18, 2026}

\begin{document}

\begin{abstract}
We study the frontier Salem--Spencer decision problem of whether a
$44$-element subset of $[1,212]$ can avoid every three-term arithmetic
progression, and use it as a scalable benchmark for constraint solving. The
benchmark combines Boolean 3-AP constraints, exact cardinality, endpoint
forcing, implied window-cardinality constraints, deterministic cube
decomposition, and independently checkable infeasibility certificates. Six
monolithic CP-SAT, HiGHS, and CDCL configurations remain unresolved after
$24$ hours. In the decomposed search, window constraints reduce the
controlled CP-SAT unresolved rate by about $28$ percentage points. A matched
split-policy ablation and an independent exhaustive cover audit expose a
cover-size versus conquer-cost tradeoff: global AP-degree reduces the complete
depth-24 cover from $12{,}582{,}912$ to $96{,}847$ cubes, but all $100$
sampled survivors time out under the historical $60$-second CP-SAT
configuration, whereas kissat closes $79$ within two hours. On $20$
byte-identical hard-pocket formulas executed sequentially on matched hardware,
CaDiCaL~3.0.0 and kissat~4.0.4 both close every instance; kissat wins all
$20$ pairs, with geometric-mean time ratio $1.477$. A two-stage CDCL survey
closes $6{,}045$ of $6{,}071$ broad residuals. Selected closures yield LRAT
certificates accepted by the formally verified \texttt{cake\_lpr} checker,
and end-to-end regressions recover the known exact values
$\rthree{80}=22$, $\rthree{90}=24$, and $\rthree{100}=27$. The unit gap
$\rthree{212}\in\{43,44\}$ remains open. We release the generators, tiered
instances, model audit, solver outputs, and verified certificates as a
reusable constraint-solving benchmark.
\end{abstract}

\maketitle

\section*{Acknowledgments}

\section*{Data and code availability}
Source code, benchmark generators, compact result tables, and manuscript
sources are available at
\url{https://github.com/memoatwit/erdos_1194/tree/constraints-submission-v1.5/erdos_r3}. Versioned solver artifacts,
including formulas, proof objects, hashes, and provenance records, are
archived at \url{https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.21413746}.

\section*{Author contributions}
M.E. conceived the study, designed and executed the computational campaign,
verified the results, developed the released software and benchmarks, and
wrote the manuscript.

\section*{Competing interests}
The author declares no competing interests.


% --- Use of generative AI ---
\section*{Use of generative AI}
In the preparation of this work, the author used Anthropic Claude
(Claude Sonnet 4.5 and Claude Opus 4.6, accessed via the Claude Code agent
interface) and OpenAI Codex (GPT-5, accessed via the Codex agent interface)
for drafting and revision assistance, code generation and review, experiment
planning, repository scaffolding, and computational orchestration. The author
made the final scientific and experimental decisions, reviewed the generated
text and code, independently checked the reported numerical and mathematical
claims, and takes full responsibility for the content. The tools do not
appear as authors.
